mappings.technique_name: Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
mappings.attack_path: Active root account access keys represent the highest-privilege credentials in AWS. If leaked, an attacker gains unrestricted control over the entire account.
Without MFA on root, a single compromised password grants full account access. Attackers can modify authentication to maintain persistence.
IAM users without MFA are vulnerable to credential stuffing and phishing. Compromised accounts provide legitimate access to cloud resources.
An IAM user with wildcard (*) permissions can perform any API action. If credentials are compromised, the attacker has unrestricted access to all AWS services.
A publicly accessible S3 bucket allows unauthenticated enumeration and download of all stored objects with no audit trail if logging is also disabled.
Public write access to S3 allows attackers to upload malicious content, deface hosted websites, or plant malware for distribution.

mappings.technique_name: Data from Cloud Storage (Combined Attack Path)
mappings.attack_path: COMBINED: Overly permissive IAM role (T1078.004) + public S3 bucket creates a complete data exfiltration chain. Attacker uses stolen credentials to enumerate all buckets and download all objects.
Unrestricted SSH (0.0.0.0/0 port 22) exposes EC2 instances to brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks from any IP on the internet.
Unrestricted RDP (0.0.0.0/0 port 3389) exposes Windows EC2 instances to credential-based attacks and BlueKeep-style exploits.
All ports open to the internet enables exploitation of any vulnerable service running on the instance.
Disabled CloudTrail means ALL API activity is unrecorded. An attacker can operate silently with no audit evidence of their actions.
COMBINED: Root access keys + disabled CloudTrail = attacker can take full control with zero audit trail. The most dangerous combination possible.
KMS keys without rotation are at higher risk of compromise. Stolen or brute-forced key material allows decryption of all protected data or re-encryption for ransom.
Without VPC Flow Logs, network-level reconnaissance and data exfiltration activity goes undetected.
